explanation_md: Execution enters at x=0, prints 0, increments x to 1, and jumps back to `start:`. This repeats for x=1 and x=2. When x becomes 3, the `if` condition is false so the loop body is skipped and `done` is printed.

prompt_md: Which restriction does C++ impose on `goto`?

options.b: `goto` cannot jump into a different function
options.c: `goto` requires the label to be declared before the `goto` statement

explanation_md: `goto` can jump both forward and backward within a function, and labels do not need to be declared before they are referenced — the compiler resolves them after parsing the entire function. The hard constraint is that `goto` cannot jump to a label in a different function; control flow is always confined to one function.
`goto end` jumps directly to the `end:` label, bypassing both the `B` and `C` output statements. Only `A` and `D` are printed.

prompt_md: Why is `goto` strongly discouraged in modern C++?

options.a: It is slower than structured control flow at runtime
options.b: It was removed from the C++20 standard
options.c: Arbitrary jumps make control flow hard to follow and reason about, and every `goto` pattern has a cleaner structured alternative
options.d: It can only jump forward, making loops impossible

explanation_md: `goto` is still valid C++ — it was not removed. Its runtime performance is no different from a branch. The real problem is readability: arbitrary jumps break the top-to-bottom mental model of reading code, and every goto pattern (loop simulation, error cleanup, skipping code) has a cleaner equivalent in structured control flow.
